coredata.dc:title: Dual-excitation and single-emission B, N-codoped carbon dots: Synthesis, fluorescent properties and multi-ion detection
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

coredata.dc:creator: Qiao, Yujie
Guo, Liulong
He, Yanyun
Li, Zheng
Meng, Qingyuan
Yao, Junyan
Zhao, Yan
Yan, Hongxia
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs) show great application prospects in various fields such as optical devices, biomedicine and sensors due to their excellent optical properties. In this work, novel B, N-codoped CDs were prepared through a facile solvothermal method using the hyperbranched polyborate as the precursor. To our surprise, the prepared B, N-codoped CDs exhibit unique “dual-excitation and single-emission” fluorescent characteristics in water–ethanol binary solvents mixtures. The B, N-codoped CDs have two excitation bands at 255 nm and 345 nm, and only one emission band at 410 nm when the water–ethanol solvent mixture ratio is 9:1. The experimental investigation shows that it is due to the fluorescence resonance energy transfer between the solvent mixture and the CDs when the ratio of water to ethanol is 9:1, which leads to the double excitation and single emission phenomenon of B, N-codoped CDs in water–ethanol solvent mixture. In addition, the prepared CDs have a unique multi-ion detection capability, and the CDs can realize the quantitative detection of Co2+ and Cu2+ as well as the qualitative detection of Fe3+. This work greatly expands the application of carbon dots in metal ion detection and provides a new perspective for the development of functional CDs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Metal ions
Solvothermal method
Fluorescence
Detection

Optical properties of B, N-codoped CDs
B, N-codoped CDs detection of metal ions

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images a, size distributions b, HR-TEM images c and X-ray diffraction spectra d of B, N-codoped CDs structures.
XPS spectra of B, N-codoped CDs and high-resolution spectra of each element.

a UV–vis spectra of B, N-codoped CDs; b The excitation-emission matrix spectra B, N-codoped CDs; c Optimum excitation/emission spectra of B, N-codoped CDs; d Fluorescence spectra of B, N-codoped CDs solution systems with different original B, N-codoped CDs solution/deionized water ratio; e Fluorescence emission spectra of dual-excited single emission B, N-codoped CDs solution at 230–270 nm excitation wavelength; f at 320–400 nm excitation wavelength.

a Fluorescence emission spectra of ethanol/water mixtures at 220–260 nm excitation wavelengths; b The spectral overlap between the emission of ethanol/water mixtures and the excitation of B, N-codoped CDs; c The emission spectra of B, N-codoped CDs and B, N-codoped CDs in mixed solvent under excitation of 255 nm; d The Emission spectra of ethanol/water mixtures and B, N-codoped CDs in mixed solvent under excitation of 255 nm.

a The effect of different metal ions on the fluorescence intensity of CDs solution under 255 nm irradiation; b Effect of different concentrations of Co2+ ions on fluorescence intensity of CDs excited by 255 nm excitation wavelength; c The linear relationship between F/F0 of CDs and Co2+ ion concentration under 255 nm irradiation; d The effect of different metal ions on the fluorescence intensity of CDs solution under 345 nm irradiation; e Effect of different concentrations of Cu2+ ions on fluorescence intensity of CDs excited by 255 nm excitation wavelength; f The linear relationship between F/F0 of CDs and Cu2+ ion concentration under 255 nm irradiation.
Schematic representation of the a mechanism and b detection process of the prepared CDs for detecting various metal ions.
The schematic illustration for the synthesis of B, N-codoped CDs.

head.ce:title: Dual-excitation and single-emission B, N-codoped carbon dots: Synthesis, fluorescent properties and multi-ion detection

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: MOE Key Laboratory of Space Applied Physics and Chemistry; Shaanxi Key Laboratory of Macromolecular Science and Technology; Xi’an Key Laboratory of Hybrid Luminescent Materials and Photonic Device, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Northwestern Polytechnical University, Xi’an 710129, China

School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: MOE Key Laboratory of Space Applied Physics and Chemistry; Shaanxi Key Laboratory of Macromolecular Science and Technology; Xi’an Key Laboratory of Hybrid Luminescent Materials and Photonic Device, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Northwestern Polytechnical University, Xi’an 710129, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Luoyang Ship Material Research Institute, Luoyang 471000, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Luoyang Ship Material Research Institute, Luoyang 471000, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs) show great application prospects in various fields such as optical devices, biomedicine and sensors due to their excellent optical properties. In this work, novel B, N-codoped CDs were prepared through a facile solvothermal method using the hyperbranched polyborate as the precursor. To our surprise, the prepared B, N-codoped CDs exhibit unique “dual-excitation and single-emission” fluorescent characteristics in water–ethanol binary solvents mixtures. The B, N-codoped CDs have two excitation bands at 255 nm and 345 nm, and only one emission band at 410 nm when the water–ethanol solvent mixture ratio is 9:1. The experimental investigation shows that it is due to the fluorescence resonance energy transfer between the solvent mixture and the CDs when the ratio of water to ethanol is 9:1, which leads to the double excitation and single emission phenomenon of B, N-codoped CDs in water–ethanol solvent mixture. In addition, the prepared CDs have a unique multi-ion detection capability, and the CDs can realize the quantitative detection of Co2+ and Cu2+ as well as the qualitative detection of Fe3+. This work greatly expands the application of carbon dots in metal ion detection and provides a new perspective for the development of functional CDs.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs), as a new member of 0-dimensional carbon nanomaterials, have attracted extensive attention in biological imaging, photoelectric devices, and sensing fields due to their superior fluorescence properties, simple preparation process, good water solubility, and excellent environmental friendliness [1–5]. So far, various methods have been developed to prepare CDs including laser irradiation approach, electrochemical exfoliation approach, hydrothermal/solvothermal method, microwave heating method, and so on [6–9]. Among them, the hydrothermal/solvothermal method is an ideal way to prepare CDs owing to the simple setup, facile preparation, various precursors, and uniform particle size [10,11]. Recent studies have shown that chemical heteroatom doping is a successful way to enhance the QY, water solubility, fluorescence properties, and other physicochemical properties of CDs, thus broadening the applications of these materials [12]. Heteroatom doping includes metal ion and nonmetal atom doping [13]. Compared to the toxic metal ions, the doping of CDs by nonmetal atoms, such as N, S, P, and B, has been more widely reported [14–17]. By introducing various atoms into the CDs, the electronic structure of the CDs can be adjusted to improve the optical properties of the CDs [18]. Among the many heteroatoms, N and B have similar atomic structures with neighboring C. Codoping of N and B can effectively modulate the physicochemical properties of CDs [19]. Boron atoms have been effectively doped into the structure of CDs due to their similar size to carbon atoms and excellent electron-absorbing ability to further improve the performance of CDs [4,20]. In addition, during the study of B and N-doped CDs, it was found that the electronegativity of boron is smaller than that of carbon, the electronegativity of nitrogen is larger than that of carbon, and the atomic radii of boron and nitrogen are similar to those of carbon. Therefore, boron and nitrogen co-doping can regulate the physicochemical properties of CDs more effectively and enhance the fluorescence properties of CDs [21]. Secondly, non-metallic elements such as B and N have less toxicity and biocompatible.

Metal ions play a vital role in nature, living organisms, and industrial processes. With the rapid development of industry, the uncontrollable discharge of heavy metal ions has seriously affected the ecological environment and human health [22,23]. Therefore, the accurate detection and quantification of the concentration, species, and dynamic changes of metal ions is of great importance. A variety of methods have been developed for ion detection, including spectroscopy, electrochemical method, nanomaterials, etc. Among these methods, carbon dots (CDs) are specially favored due to their excellent fluorescence properties and water solubility. As early as 2010, Mao et al. reported the detection of Cu2+ using CDs as fluorescent probes for the first time [24]. Subsequently, various CDs have been designed and used for the detection of various metal ions, such as Fe3+, Cu2+, Al3+, Hg2+, Cd2+, and Co2+, based on the their designability of surface groups and fluorescence properties [25–27]. Wang et al. fulfilled high sensitivity and selectivity towards Cu2+ with a LOD of 0.226 μM using CDs aqueous solution [28]. Recently, Gao et al. prepared a red emission CDs, which can be used as a fluorescent probe for the detection of Pt2+, Au3+, and Pd2+ [29]. Li et al. fabricated four types of carbon dots (CDs) using carboxymethyl nanocellulose as a carbon source doped with different elements using a hydrothermal method, the CDs-N exhibited better sensitivity and selectivity for Hg2+ detection, with a low limit of detection of 8.29 μM [30]. Although these CDs perform well, they it is not possible to differentiate between specific metal ions by the change of fluorescence intensity, and if more than two metal ions all produce fluorescence response signals there is an inability to differentiate, which limits the further application of CD in metal ion sensors. Therefore, it is important to develop a novel fluorescent CDs with distinguishable response signals for different metal ions. Hence, we prepared novel B, N-codoped CDs with outstanding fluorescent properties for the detection of multiple metal ions using hydrothermal/solvothermal method.

Herein, a novel B, N-codoped CDs were prepared via a facile solvothermal method using hyperbranched polyborate as the precursor. To our surprise, the prepared CDs have unique “dual-excitation and single-emission” characteristics in aqueous solution, and exhibit different fluorescence signals when adding metal ions. It showed a yellow–brown flocculent precipitate when adding Fe3+, a decrease in fluorescent intensity at 255 nm and 345 nm excitation with the appearance of Co2+, fluorescent intensity at 345 nm excitation for Cu2+. Based on the peculiar phenomenon of CDs, detection of Fe3+, Co2+, and Cu2+ were achieved.

All chemical reagents used in this study were bought from Macklin and used directly without further purification. The NMR (400 MHz) spectra and Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were measured by a Bruker Avance 400 MHz spectrometer and a NICOLET 5700 FTIR spectrometer, respectively. The UV–vis absorption spectra, fluorescence spectra, fluorescence lifetime, and absolute quantum yield were tested using a Shimadzu UV-2500 spectrophotometer, a Hitachi F-4600 fluorescence spectrophotometer, and a steady/transient-state fluorescence spectrometer (FLS980, Edinburgh Instruments), respectively. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were recorded by an FEI Tecnai G2 F20 microscope. Imaging X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were conducted on a K-Alpha spectrometer (Axis Ultra, Kratos Analytical Ltd., U.K.). Powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) measurement was conducted on an X’Pert Pro Pro Powder diffractometer from PANalytical (Cu Kα radiation, 40 kV, 40 mA) (D/Max2550VB+/PC).

The synthesis of hyperbranched polyborates based on transesterification Polycondensation [10,31,32]. Firstly, the mixtures of tributyl borate (0.20 mol) and N-methyldiethanolamine (0.47 mol, for P1) or diethylene glycol (0.47 mol, for P2) were added to a 250 mL three-necked flask equipped with a mechanical stirrer, nitrogen inlet, and condenser tube. The mixtures were heated to 130 °C and kept at this temperature in the atmosphere of nitrogen until the byproduct (n-butyl alcohol) was distilled off. Successively, the temperature of the mixtures was slowly raised to the maximum of 180 °C. At the same time, the distillate temperature was kept below 112 °C. Then the mixtures were heated at 180 °C until the distillate temperature was below 50 °C. Sequentially, the mixtures were cooled to room temperature and the precursor hyperbranched polyborates (P1 and P2) were obtained. Then the CDs were prepared through a simple hydrothermal/solvothermal method using the prepared hyperbranched polyborate (P1 or P2) as the precursor [33], as shown in Scheme 1 , and the detailed process was shown in the Supplementary information (SI).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of B, N-codoped CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescence is one of the most important properties of CDs, which is also a necessary precondition for their application in ions detections. We firstly systematically studied the optimal synthesis conditions of CDs, including precursor structures, reaction solvents, and reaction temperatures. The results as shown in Fig. S2. By comparing the fluorescent intensity of CDs, the synthesis conditions were optimized as follows: P1 was selected as the precursor, and the solvent and temperature were determined as ethanol and 220 °C, respectively. In the subsequent experiments, the CDs were prepared under the optimal conditions were named B, N-codoped CDs and used for further experiments.

The morphology and size distributions of the B, N-codoped CDs were investigated via transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM). As shown in Fig. 1 a, the CDs presented a near-spherical structure with uniform size which were uniformly dispersed without agglomeration. The sizes of the CDs are 2.6 ± 0.4 nm (Fig. 1b). Then, the HR-TEM image (Fig. 1c) of CDs shows that these CDs have no obvious lattice fringe, showing an amorphous state. Subsequently, X-ray diffraction (XRD) was used to characterize the prepared CDs, and the result is shown in Fig. 1d. The XRD spectrum shows a wide peak around 17.3° [34]. As shown in Fig. S3, in the Raman spectrum, the two major peaks near 1368 cm−1 and 1596 cm−1 correspond to the D band and G band, respectively. The D band is the out of plane vibration of sp2 carbon due to the presence of structural defects and the G band is the result of the in plane vibration of the sp2 carbon atoms [35]. The relative intensity of the ID/IG is 1.51. The XRD spectrum and Raman spectra this further proves that the prepared CDs are amorphous. The sizes of the prepared B, N-codoped CDs were further studied by dynamic light-scattering instrument (DLS). According to Fig. S4, the measured CD sizes are about 4.5 nm, which is larger than that calculated in TEM image. It could be caused by size variation in solution state and solid state. In solutions, the tested sizes are the hydrodynamic diameters of CDs, including the nucleus and surrounding micellar structures. On the contrary, only nucleus are tested in solid state, thus resulting in decreased sizes. Both results demonstrated that the synthesized CDs are smaller than 10 nm, which can be used as fluorescent probes for the detection of metal ions.

The prepared CDs were then characterized using Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) and nuclear magnetic resonance spectroscopy (Fig. S5). Furthermore, to explore the element compositions and valance state of CDs, the X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was used [36–38]. In the XPS spectra of B, N-codoped CDs (Fig. 2 ), four elements can be seen at 532.1, 400.9, 286.0, and 192.0 eV, which were corresponding to O 1s, N 1s, C 1s, and B 1s, respectively. In the narrow scan spectra of O 1s, there are two peaks appears, with the binding energies of 534.2 and 532.5 eV, respectively, which could be caused by O–C and O–B bond. The high-resolution N 1s spectra can be deconvoluted into C–N (399.6 eV) and pyrrolic N (401.2 eV). The detailed scan spectra of C 1s show three dominant peaks at 285.4, 286.4, and 289.6 eV, corresponding to C–N, C–O, and π–π*, respectively. The B 1s spectra display one signals peak at 192.0 eV, which is related to the B–O bond. The XPS results further indicated the successful formation of the B, N-codoped CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties of B, N-codoped CDs
ce:section.ce:para: To study the optical properties of the prepared B, N-codoped CDs, UV–vis absorption spectra were tested. The absorption peak at 226 nm might be attributed to the π–π* transition of C=C and the peak at 285 nm was assigned to the n–σ* transition of –OH of B, N-codoped CDs (Fig. 3 a) [25]. Then, we investigated the fluorescence properties of the B, N-codoped CDs. As shown in Fig. 3(b–c), the optimal excitation/emission wavelength of the CDs is 370/470 nm. In addition, the emission wavelength red-shifted with the increase of excitation wavelength, showing the typical excitation-dependent emission characteristics. The absolute fluorescence quantum yield and fluorescence lifetime of B, N-codoped CDs are 6.43 % and 4.83 ns, respectively, indicating that the prepared B, N-codoped CDs has obvious fluorescent features (Fig. S6).

Furthermore, we explored the fluorescence properties of CDs in aqueous solution. With the increase of water content, the optimal excitation/emission wavelengths of CDs have a blue shift. An obvious increase in 400 nm emission peak was observed when the water content reaches 4 mL, and the CDs solution had better fluorescence when the water content was 4.5 mL (Fig. 3d). To our surprise, the B, N-codoped CDs have two excitation peaks in ethanol–water mixtures solution. Subsequently, we further explored the mechanism for the dual-excitation and single-emission phenomena in CDs solution. It was found that when the ratio of ethanol to water was 1:9, the mixed solvent possesses obvious fluorescence emission at 255/310 nm (Fig. 4 a). We infer that there might be fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) between the mixed solvent and CDs. Actually, it needs to meet several requirements to achieve efficient FRET, including a acceptor and a small distance between the donor and acceptor (less than 10 nm) [39].

Herein, we compare the emission spectra of ethanol–water mixtures with the excitation spectra of B, N-codoped CDs. As shown in Fig. 4b, the excitation peaks of B, N-codoped CDs overlap to a large extent with the emission peaks of the ethanol/water mixture. In addition, the emission intensity of the B, N-codoped CDs was enhanced in the ethanol/water mixed solvent, whereas the emission intensity of the ethanol/water mixed solvent was decreased (Fig. 4c–d). Limited by computational methods and capabilities, we could not know the small distance between the donor and acceptor, but the results of these experiments suggest that there may be FRET between the ethanol/water mixed solvents. When the CDs solution is excited by 255 nm excitation wavelength, the mixed solvent is excited and generates fluorescence emission at 310 nm. Then, the CDs is excited by 310 nm emission [40]. When the excitation wavelength is 345 nm, the CDs was directly excited and generates fluorescence emission at 410 nm. Thus, “dual- excitation and single-emission” fluorescence phenomenon was observed. This characteristic is significantly different from the reported CDs systems, and the unusual dual-excitation and single-emission phenomenon has unique advantages in ion detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: B, N-codoped CDs detection of metal ions
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesized B, N-codoped CDs were used for metal ions detection. The responsiveness of B, N-codoped CDs to Al3+, Ba2+, Ca2+, Cd2+, Co2+, Cu2+, Fe3+, K+, Na+, and Zn2+ were shown in Fig. 5 . The CDs show distinct responsiveness towards Fe3+, Co2+ and Cu2+. When Fe3+ (10-3M) was added, flocculent precipitation was appeared in the solution, which can be used for the qualitative detect of Fe3+ (Fig. S7).

For the reason of the precipitates, we speculated that it was due to the coordination of Fe3+ with B, N-codoped CDs. To confirm our assumption, we used XPS to analyze the coordination of the samples. As shown in Fig. S7b, when FeCl3 was added to the B, N-codoped CDs solution, the precipitates have higher Fe 2p3/2 and Fe 2p1/2 peaks (711.0 and 724.2 eV) with higher binding energy compared to pure FeCl3 (710.4 and 723.5 eV), indicating that the interaction between FeCl3 and B, N-codoped CDs [41]. To further identify the exact coordination atoms in B, N-codoped CDs, the N 1s XPS spectra of the precipitates were analyzed. According to Fig. S7c, the spectra can be convolved to pyridine-N (402.1 eV), Fe-N (399.5 eV), and C-N (398.3 eV) [42]. The Fe-N bond indicates that the nitrogen atom acts as a ligand atom to coordinate with Fe3+. The O 1s XPS spectra of the precipitates also shows four peaks at 533.1, 531.7, 530.6 and 529.2 eV. (Fig. S7d), corresponding to B-O-Fe, C-O-Fe, B-O, and C-O, respectively. It is worth noting that the B-O (530.6 eV) and C-O (529.2 eV) in the B, N-codoped CDs/Fe3+ have lower binding energies than those in B, N-codoped CDs(534.2 and 532.5 eV), suggesting that the electron transfer from O to Fe [43].

Different from the precipitation with Fe3+, the B, N-codoped CDs solution has different fluorescence signals for Al3+, Ba2+, Ca2+, Cd2+, Cd2+, Co2+, Cu2+, K+, Na+, and Zn2+. As shown in the Fig. 5a, when λEx is 255 nm, the fluorescence intensity of the CDs solutions were obviously quenched with the appearance of Co2+ or Cu2+, and the quenching rate of Co2+ reaches 98 %. When λEx is 345 nm, the fluorescence intensity of the CDs solution can be quenched by Co2+. Therefore, Co2+ has a quenching effect on CDs solution both at 255 nm and 345 nm excitation wavelengths. However, the fluorescence intensity is slightly influenced with other ions at the excitation wavelength of 345 nm. We believe that the addition of Co2+and Cu2+ to the CDs solution could affect the excitation of the mixed solvent (ethanol:water = 1:9) at which in turn leads to the fluorescence quench of the CDs solution at 410 nm. Further, experiments were designed to validate the inference. As shown in Fig. S8, it can be seen that the fluorescence intensity at 310 nm of the mixed solvents is significantly reduced when 10-3 M of Co2+ or Cu2+ is added, proving the above conclusion.

In view of the remarkable quenching effect of Co2+ on CDs solution at 255 nm and 345 nm excitation wavelengths, we tested the effect of Co2+ concentration on the quenching efficiency. When excited by 255 nm, Co2+ ions can change the fluorescence intensity of CDs solution with a concentration as low as 10 μM (as shown in Fig. 5b). Meanwhile, there was a good linear relationship (R2 = 0.9910) between the F/F0 of CDs solution and the concentration of Co2+ in the range of 10–100 μM, with a LOD of 5.49 μM (3σ) (Fig. 5c). Similarly, when the excitation wavelength is 345 nm, the F/F0 of CDs solution also displays a good linear relationship (R2 = 0.9941) versus the concentration of Co2+ in the range of 0 to 1000 μM, and the LOD (3σ) was 38.58 μM (Fig. S12a–b) [44].Therefore, these CDs have the potential to be used as a novel Co2+ probe with Co2+.

The photostability of carbon dots was subsequently explored, we selected the concentration of carbon dots applied for ion detection for photostability testing, after which the fluorescence intensity of the B, N-codoped CDs solution was tested for the change of fluorescence intensity by continuous irradiation with the excitation wavelengths of 345 nm and 255 nm for 9000 s (i.e., 2.5 h). The results are as Fig. S9, it can be seen that the fluorescence intensity of the carbon dots remained above 97 % during the test and there was no obvious decay trend, indicating that the prepared B, N-codoped CDs have excellent photostability.

In addition, we tested the relationship between the concentration of Cu2+ ions and the fluorescence quenching efficiency of CDs at an excitation wavelength of 255 nm. The F/F0 of the CDs solution showed a good linear relationship with the Cu2+ concentration in the range of 10 ∼ 100 μM (R2 = 0.9944), and the LOD (3σ) was 6.73 μM (Fig. 5e–f) [44]. The pH of the sample affects the fluorescence properties of the probe by changing the surface chemistry of CDs, so we tested the changes in the detection of Co2+ versus Cu2+ by carbon point solutions at different pH conditions. The fluorescence intensities of the B, N-codoped CDs with Co2+ and Cu2+ at the original pH conditions were used as a reference, then the relative values of the fluorescence intensities of the solutions at each pH condition are shown in Fig. S10. We can see that when the carbon dots solution is in the weakly acidic and weakly alkaline range of pH in the detection of Co2+ or Cu2+, its fluorescence intensity does not change much compared with that of the original sample, which shows a strong fluorescence stability. And when the pH is 1 or 13 (when the solution is more acidic and basic), its fluorescence intensity has a more obvious change.

We explored the mechanism of fluorescence quenching in B, N-codoped CDs solutions before and after Co2+ and Cu2+ treatments. We tested the Zeta potential versus DLS spectra of solutions of B, N-codoped CDs before and after Co2+ and Cu2+ treatments. As shown in Fig S11, DLS shows that the hydrodynamic diameter of the CDs increases dramatically from 4.5 nm to 58.8 nm after the addition of Co2+ ions (Fig. S11b), and that the zeta potential of the CDs solution also increases from −11.9 to +25.1 mV (Fig. S11d), both of which may be attributed to the coordination of Co2+ with functional groups (–OH, –COOH) on the surface of the carbon dots and the strong interaction with doping elements such as N [45]. In addition, we tested the fluorescence lifetimes of the B, N-codoped CDs solutions before and after the addition of Co2+ (Fig. S12c–d), we can see that there is almost no difference in their fluorescence lifetime, the results further confirm that the detection of Co2+ ions is a static quenching process [46,47].

Compared with the sharp increase in the hydrodynamic diameter of the CDs solution with the addition of Co2+ ions, the change in the hydrodynamic diameter of the CDs solution with the addition of Cu2+ ions is not significant (Fig. S11c), and the zeta potential of the CDs solution increases from −11.9 to −9.18 mV (Fig. S11d), which is a smaller change. It is speculated that the Cu2+ will not interact with the carbon dots at the excitation wavelength of 255 nm, but will quench the emission of the ethanol–water mixture at 310 nm, leading to the fluorescence quenching of the carbon dots solution at 410 nm.

Furthermore, based on the above analysis, we could detect different metal ions using the above-mentioned CDs solution (the ethanol–water ratio is 1:9), and the detection principle and process are shown in Fig. 6 . To compare the advantages of the application of B, N-codoped CDs in metal ion detection, CDs with similar applications are studied and the results are shown in Table S-1 [48–54]. The CDs prepared in this work achieved the detection of multiple metal ions, which greatly improved the detection efficiency of carbonized polymer dots in metal sample detection.

In conclusion, a novel B, N-codoped CDs have been prepared through a facile solvothermal method using hyperbranched polyborate as the precursor. B, N-codoped CDs in the water–ethanol mixed solution gradually appeared the excitation band at 255 nm with the increase of water content, showing the “dual-excitation and single-emission” characteristics. The reason for this was explored and it was found that when the ratio of water to ethanol was 9:1, the mixed solvent had an emission band at 255 nm/310 nm, and at this time, there was a fluorescence resonance energy transfer between the mixed solvent and the CDs, leading to the phenomenon of double-excitation-single-emission of the B, N codoped CDs in the water–ethanol solvent mixtures. Based on the special phenomenon of CDs, the application of CDs in metal ion detection is studied. The CDs can realize the quantitative detection of Co2+ and Cu2+, with a limit of detection (LOD) of 5.49 μM for Co2+ and 6.73 μM for Cu2+. In addition, qualitative detection of Fe3+ can be achieved due to the coordination effect on Fe3+ and CDs. This work realizes the detection of multiple metal ions using a CDs solution and greatly improves their detection efficiency.

Yujie Qiao: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Liulong Guo: Writing – review & editing, Methodology, Data curation. Yanyun He: Supervision. Zheng Li: Supervision. Qingyuan Meng: Supervision. Junyan Yao: Supervision, Data curation. Yan Zhao: Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Hongxia Yan: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China [22175143]. And the authors thank the Analytical and Testing Center of Northwestern Polytechnical University for test assistance.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jphotochem.2024.116180.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Engineering white light-emitting diodes with high color rendering index from biomass carbonized polymer dots
J. Colloid Interface Sci.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: W. Meng, B. Wang, L. Ai, H. Song, S. Lu, Engineering white light-emitting diodes with high color rendering index from biomass carbonized polymer dots, J. Colloid Interface Sci. 598 (2021) 274-282.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Electrophoretic analysis and purification of fluorescent single-walled carbon nanotube fragments
